Growing concerns about racial bias in pulse oximeters in adults highlight the need for objective assessment of skin color to investigate if skin pigmentation contributes to the inaccuracy. While skin color scales and cutaneous colorimetric devices have been utilized in adult dermatology for various applications, their use and effectiveness in neonates and infants are not well understood. Objective classification and quantification of skin color in these populations are crucial for a better understanding of the inaccuracies of skin transmittance\u2010based devices like pulse oximetry. This state\u2010of\u2010the\u2010art review provides methods and findings from studies assessing neonatal skin color and pigmentation in objective and quantifiable ways, focusing on age\u2010dependent and site\u2010specific effects. Our goal is to provide a comprehensive overview of current knowledge regarding objective assessment of skin pigmentation, particularly melanin. We aim to guide future research efforts aimed at enhancing the accuracy of pigment measurements in medical devices, especially concerning neonates and
